narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 45.5% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades, which is notably lower than the typical fifth or sixth decile among students who went on to pass the bar. That early signal of relative performance in later undergraduate years carried the strongest weight in the model's thinking for your profile. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50 stood out as a key strength, well above the 3.24 average for bar passers, and it significantly pulled the prediction back toward success. Your LSAT score of 32 also helped in that direction, though not as powerfully, offering some reassurance compared to typical admitted students. Meanwhile, elements like your high family income bracket aligned with a smaller but successful subset of passers, and your full-time study plans matched the norm for those who succeed. Overall, your Year 3 decile ranking overshadowed these positives, signaling to the model a tougher path to bar passage than for most accepted applicants, whose stronger later-year standings tipped their profiles toward approval. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid undergrad record and other promising aspects. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the 37 average of bar passers, or strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or grad-level performance. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for full-time students from varied backgrounds. Many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements—your path isn't closed, just calling for strategic next steps.
